## Why sequence matters

Classical bag-of-words methods ignore order. That is useful for many business
tasks, but it cannot answer a basic question: given the tokens we have already
seen, what token is likely to come next?

`GRA 4164` uses n-gram models to introduce that shift from unordered text
representation to sequential prediction. The model is simple enough to inspect by
hand, yet close enough to modern language modelling that it forms a practical
bridge to later LLM topics.

## An n-gram is a short-memory model

An n-gram model predicts the next token from the previous `n - 1` tokens.

- A **unigram** model ignores context and uses only overall token frequencies.
- A **bigram** model uses one previous token.
- A **trigram** model uses two previous tokens.

The trade-off is direct. Larger `n` gives richer local context, but also creates
many more possible histories to estimate from data. In small corpora, that quickly
leads to sparse counts and unstable probabilities.

## Bigram probabilities come from counts

The cleanest first example is a character-level bigram model. Add a start token and
an end token to each observed string, then count every adjacent pair:

- `<S> -> v`
- `v -> e`
- `e -> g`
- `g -> a`
- `a -> r`
- `r -> d`
- `d -> <E>`

For a bigram model, the conditional probability of the next character is:

$$
P(c_t \mid c_{t-1}) =
\frac{\operatorname{count}(c_{t-1}, c_t)}{\operatorname{count}(c_{t-1})}
$$

This is the core idea behind next-token prediction: estimate a probability
distribution for the next step from observed transitions in the training corpus.

## A count matrix makes the model visible

In practice, it is useful to store the counts in a matrix. Each row represents the
current token and each column represents the next token. Normalizing each row turns
counts into a probability distribution.

That makes two things easy to inspect:

- which tokens are likely to begin a sequence,
- and which continuations are likely after a given token.

The original lecture notebook uses a names corpus and constructs this matrix
explicitly before sampling new names from the learned probabilities.

## Smoothing prevents zero-probability dead ends

Pure count estimates are brittle. If a token pair never appears in the training
data, the estimated probability becomes zero. Then one unseen transition makes the
probability of a full sequence collapse to zero as well.

A standard fix is additive smoothing:

$$
\hat{P}(c_t \mid c_{t-1}) =
\frac{\operatorname{count}(c_{t-1}, c_t) + 1}
{\sum_j \bigl(\operatorname{count}(c_{t-1}, j) + 1\bigr)}
$$

This does not solve every sparsity problem, but it is enough to explain why
language models need some way to handle unseen events.

::: {.teacher-only}
Ask students why a deterministic "most likely next character" rule produces poor
generated text. The point is to distinguish probability estimation from greedy
prediction before introducing sampling.
:::

## Sampling turns the model into a generator

Once each row is a probability distribution, generation is straightforward:

1. Start from `<S>`.
2. Sample the next token from the row for the current token.
3. Move to the sampled token and repeat.
4. Stop when `<E>` is generated.

This is why even a simple bigram model can generate plausible-looking names or
short text fragments. It has learned local sequential structure, even though it has
no deeper understanding of meaning.

## Log likelihood measures model fit

To evaluate the model, we ask how much probability it assigns to the sequences that
actually occur in the data. For a corpus of observed transitions, the log
likelihood is:

$$
\ell = \sum_{w \in D} \sum_{t=0}^{T_w} \log P(c_{t+1} \mid c_t)
$$

Higher log likelihood means the model fits the observed data better. In practice we
usually minimize the negative average log likelihood, which turns the objective into
a loss function.

This matters because later neural language models use the same logic: they are
still trained to assign high probability to the next token that actually occurred.

## What to report

When students describe an n-gram language model in this course, they should report:

- what the token unit is (character or word),
- what value of `n` is being used and what context it retains,
- how counts were converted into probabilities,
- whether smoothing was applied, and
- one honest limitation of the model, especially around short memory or data
  sparsity.
